findings.summary: Origin Investments acquired Queens Wedgewood-Houston (221-unit Class A multifamily) for $79.4M+ in Feb 2025
Houston-based developer paid $6.5M for a WeHo development site
27 active properties for sale with average listing price $749,981 as of March 2026
Strong institutional buyer presence with Origin, AJ Capital, Atlas Real Estate Partners all active in the submarket
Wedgewood Village: AJ Capital 18-acre, 1.6M SF mixed-use mega-project broken ground across all components
Memoir May Hosiery: 109 residential units opening March 2026
430 Chestnut: 140,000 SF Class A office building under development
Delux Weho mixed-use development at Humphreys & 4th Ave South targeting 2026 completion
Merritt Mansion Lawn site: office and multifamily buildings under construction
500-516 Hagan St: 4-building mixed-use project up to 7 stories planned
Metro-wide: units under construction down 25%, permits down 50% — supply pipeline thinning
Nashville ranked #2 US metro for job growth and income (Aug 2025)
~24,000 new jobs forecast for 2026; healthcare, tech, logistics, and creative sectors leading
38,000+ international immigrants since 2020; metro population 1.35M growing at 1.28% annually
GEODIS Park (30,109-seat MLS stadium) at adjacent Fairgrounds is a major neighborhood catalyst with new multimodal transit connections
Amazon, Oracle, Meta expanding Nashville-area operations; HCA and Vanderbilt anchor healthcare employment
Nashville average asking rents $1,643 (Nov 2025), down 0.5% trailing 3-month but expected to reach ~$1,700 by year-end
Stabilized asset occupancy ticked up 30bps YoY to 94.3% in Oct 2025
Annual rent growth projected at 2.1% by Q4 2025; modest improvement expected in 2026 as supply wave absorbs
Deliveries projected to fall for third consecutive year in 2026, supporting rent recovery
National CMBS issuance hit $115.2B in 2025 — highest since 2007
CMBS delinquency rate 7.14% (Feb 2026), down from 7.47% in Jan — improving but still elevated
$100B+ CMBS loans maturing in 2026, mostly office — creates refinancing pressure but also buying opportunities
Nashville CRE market maturing; capital becoming more selective with pricing stabilizing
Tennessee statewide prohibition on rent control — Nashville cannot enact local rent stabilization
Metro Planning Commission passed new zoning codes diversifying housing types (Sep 2025); WeHo properties up-zoned to 3-4 units
Wedgewood-Houston/Chestnut Hill Planning Study completed by Metro Nashville Planning Department
STR non-owner-occupied permits frozen since 2022 in residential zones — redirects investment toward long-term rentals

key_findings: WeHo is in a supply-heavy transitional phase with 1.6M+ SF of Wedgewood Village alone, but metro-wide pipeline is contracting sharply (permits -50%), suggesting a favorable entry window for 2027+ absorption
Institutional capital is active and confident — Origin's $79.4M multifamily acquisition at $359K/door signals strong conviction in the submarket's trajectory
GEODIS Park and the Fairgrounds multimodal infrastructure have fundamentally improved WeHo's connectivity and catalyzed adjacent development
Nashville's #2 ranking for job growth and income, combined with 24,000 new jobs forecast for 2026, provides strong demand underpinning for all asset classes
Regulatory environment is among the most landlord-friendly in major US metros: no rent control possible, pro-density zoning reforms, and STR restrictions that benefit long-term rental operators
Near-term risk: significant localized supply could pressure rents and occupancy through mid-2027 until absorption catches up
Capital markets are selectively available — strong CMBS issuance but lenders are more discriminating on underwriting, favoring sponsors with track records in growing submarkets like WeHo

feature_gaps.description: No access to Davidson County deed transfer records for real-time transaction tracking at the submarket level
CoStar/Yardi Matrix submarket-level rent and occupancy data not available through web search — only metro-level aggregates found
Nashville building permit API (ArcGIS hub) could not be programmatically queried for WeHo-specific permit volume
Unable to compute absorption rate vs. delivery pace for the submarket without unit-level pipeline and leasing velocity data
No spatial map overlay of development pipeline projects relative to GEODIS Park and transit improvements
BLS employment data available at MSA level only — cannot isolate job growth within Davidson County or WeHo submarket
No cap rate compression/expansion model for WeHo relative to broader Nashville CBD — would need historical transaction data
Opportunity Zone capital flows into WeHo census tracts not trackable through public search

scoring_weights_used.note: Default weights used — config/scoring_weights.json not yet created
